topic: grandfather's story — planting the first trees

turns.content: I want to write something down my friend. about my grandfather. the story of how he started our farm. I tell my children but I want it written so it does not die with me. can you help me shape the words

That is a meaningful thing to do. Preserving your grandfather's story in writing ensures it survives for generations. I'd be happy to help you shape it.

Tell me the story the way you would tell it to your children. Speak naturally, and I will help you organize it into something that reads well on paper. We can keep your voice in it — it should sound like you.

Start from the beginning. How did your grandfather come to plant the first cocoa trees?

he was a young man. maybe 20 years. he had never seen cocoa before. a trader passed through the village from the coast and showed him the pods. my grandfather said the pods looked like something Nyame had carved by hand. he asked the trader for seeds

That's a beautiful image — pods that looked carved by God's hand. That tells you something about your grandfather right away. He saw beauty and opportunity in the same moment.

Go on. Did the trader give him the seeds? What happened next?

the trader said he would bring seeds on his next journey. 3 months later he came back with a small bag. maybe 50 seeds. my grandfather cleared a piece of forest near the stream. everyone in the village laughed at him. they said why are you planting something you cannot eat. we grow yam and cassava not this strange thing

So he went against what everyone thought was sensible. That takes real courage — especially in a farming community where people grow what they know and trust.

The detail about 50 seeds and clearing land by the stream is important. It shows he was practical too. He chose land near water because he understood the trees would need moisture.

Did any of those first 50 seeds survive? What happened when the trees began to grow?

he said maybe 30 survived. the rest the animals ate or they did not germinate. but those 30 trees. my friend when they flowered for the first time after 3 years he called the whole village to come and see. small white flowers on the trunk. the people stopped laughing then

What a moment. Three years of patience and doubt, and then those small white flowers proved him right. Calling the whole village to see — that wasn't boasting. That was sharing. He wanted them to understand what was possible.

This is the heart of the story. A man who planted something nobody believed in and waited three years for the proof.

What happened after the first harvest? Did others in the village start planting cocoa too?

yes. when the first pods ripened and he broke them open and dried the beans and a buyer came from Kumasi and paid him cash money. real money my friend. more than yam could ever give. then everyone wanted seeds from him. he gave freely. he said the rain does not fall on one roof alone

That proverb coming from him — your grandfather — and still alive in your family today. That is powerful.

He could have kept the advantage to himself. Instead he shared the seeds and changed the whole village. That is a legacy worth writing down.

Let me put what you've told me into a shape. Something like this:

---

*When my grandfather was a young man of twenty, he had never seen a cocoa pod. A trader from the coast passed through our village carrying pods that my grandfather said looked like something Nyame had carved by hand. He asked the trader for seeds.*

*Three months later the trader returned with a small bag — maybe fifty seeds. My grandfather cleared a piece of forest near the stream and planted them. The village laughed. They said: why do you plant something you cannot eat? We grow yam and cassava, not this strange thing.*

*Of the fifty, maybe thirty survived. For three years he waited. When the first small white flowers appeared on the trunks, he called the whole village to come and see. The people stopped laughing.*

*When the pods ripened and a buyer came from Kumasi and paid cash money — more than yam could ever bring — everyone wanted seeds. My grandfather gave freely. He said: the rain does not fall on one roof alone.*

---

How does that feel? Do you want to add or change anything?

that is good my friend. very good. but you must add one thing. he always said to my father and my father said to me. he said when you plant a tree you are making a promise to your grandchildren. I want that in there

That line is everything. It ties the whole story together — because here you are, his grandchild, keeping that promise.

Here is the ending with that added:

---

*When the pods ripened and a buyer came from Kumasi and paid cash money — more than yam could ever bring — everyone wanted seeds. My grandfather gave freely. He said: the rain does not fall on one roof alone.*

*He told my father, and my father told me: when you plant a tree, you are making a promise to your grandchildren.*

*He kept his promise. The trees still stand.*

---

I added that last short line because it is true. Would you like me to write out the full piece from beginning to end so you can copy it down?
yes write the full thing. beginning to end. so I can copy exactly

Here is the full piece:

---

*When my grandfather was a young man of twenty, he had never seen a cocoa pod. A trader from the coast passed through our village carrying pods that my grandfather said looked like something Nyame had carved by hand. He asked the trader for seeds.*

*Three months later the trader returned with a small bag — maybe fifty seeds. My grandfather cleared a piece of forest near the stream and planted them. The village laughed. They said: why do you plant something you cannot eat? We grow yam and cassava, not this strange thing.*

*Of the fifty, maybe thirty survived. For three years he waited. When the first small white flowers appeared on the trunks, he called the whole village to come and see. The people stopped laughing.*

*When the pods ripened and a buyer came from Kumasi and paid cash money — more than yam could ever bring — everyone wanted seeds. My grandfather gave freely. He said: the rain does not fall on one roof alone.*

*He told my father, and my father told me: when you plant a tree, you are making a promise to your grandchildren.*

*He kept his promise. The trees still stand.*

---

That is the full story. Copy it carefully.
the trees still stand. yes. that is right. medaase my friend. I will write this in a book for my children. when I am gone they will read it and know where they come from

That is a gift they will carry their whole lives. Knowing where you come from gives you strength for where you are going.

If you ever want to add more stories — about your father, or about your own early days on the farm — I am here to help you shape those too. A family needs its stories written down. Good evening, Kofi.
